macro_segments.title: Introduction to Zika (quasi-marital connection) and the case of marrying the mother when the yevama dies

micro_segments.title: Opening review of previous discussion on yibum and its connection to inheritance
Introduction to the concept of zika as a quasi-marital connection in yibum law
The case of Reuven, Rachel, and Shimon: can the yavam marry the yevama's mother after her death?
Interpretation of the distinction between ein zika and yesh zika positions
Distinction between zika (biblical concept) and maamar (rabbinic institution)

macro_segments.title: The case of two brothers and the question of whether zika applies when one becomes engaged to the sister

micro_segments.title: Setup: Reuven and Levi married to sisters Rachel and Leah; Reuven dies; Levi becomes engaged to Leah but hasn't consummated
The problem: can Levi consummate marriage with Leah without dealing with Rachel first?
Why the mishna specifies maamar rather than relying on zika alone
Clarification that zika creates a quasi-marital relationship even without formal maamar

macro_segments.title: The case of ashes achiv shelo haya baolamo: when the brother was not yet born when the husband died

micro_segments.title: Setup: Reuven married to Rachel; Reuven dies; later Levi is born; Shimon was alive and could do yibum
Question: if Shimon merely has zika without maamar, can Levi do yibum with Sarah (Shimon's wife)?
Proof from braisa: when only zika exists (no maamar), Levi can do yibum with Sarah
Interpretation: the braisa shows ein zika l'achar mita (zika doesn't persist after death)

macro_segments.title: The case of four brothers and two sisters: testing whether zika is meaningful in multiple-brother scenarios

micro_segments.title: Setup: four brothers; Reuven and Levi married to sisters Rachel and Leah; both husbands die; surviving brothers want to do yibum
The problem: if both survivors do yibum, one with Rachel and one with Leah, they commit achot ishah
Resolution: both must do chalitza, not yibum, to avoid potential bitul mitzvat yevamin if one dies
Analysis: this case shows Rabbi Meir holds ein zika, and the concern is bitul mitzvat yevamin, not zika

macro_segments.title: The position of Rabban Gamliel: ein zika and permissibility of bitul mitzvat yevamin

micro_segments.title: Case: Reuven married Rachel; Levi married underage Leah; Reuven dies; what should Levi do?
Rabban Gamliel's ruling: if Leah does mi'un, good; if not, wait until she grows up, then Rachel becomes erva
Proof of ein zika from Rabban Gamliel: permits continued relations with Leah despite zika to Rachel
Comparison: Rabban Gamliel holds both ein zika and is unconcerned with bitul mitzvat yevamin
The gemara argues these two positions may not be logically connected

macro_segments.title: The positions of Rav and Shmuel: Rav says ein zika (muttar to marry mother), Shmuel says yesh zika (assur)

micro_segments.title: Attribution of the position about marrying the mother to Shmuel (yesh zika) rather than Rav
Shmuel's consistency: holds yesh zika in both the mother case and the sister case (Levi/Leah)
Citation of explicit tradition: Rav Yehuda in the name of Shmuel holds yesh zika
Conclusion: the gemara holds yesh zika broadly—even after death and with multiple brothers

topical_tags.term: asur levatel mitzvos yevamin (cannot nullify the mitzvah of yibum)
ashes achiv shelo haya baolamo (wife of brother not yet born)
mi'un (rejection of marriage by underage woman)
shomeres yavam (woman waiting for yibum or chalitza)
